questions.stem: According to the course introduction, what is the primary focus of this course?
options: The internals of building LLMs: loss functions, sigmoid activations, and training epochs
How to effectively use LLMs, with a special focus on software engineering tasks
Hardware acceleration and GPU infrastructure for training neural networks
Classical software engineering methodologies that do not involve AI
questions.explanation: The lecture explicitly states that building LLMs (neural networks, loss functions, embeddings, etc.) is NOT the focus; the course centers on effectively using LLMs, especially turning specifications into code and software systems.

questions.stem: The lecture organizes the course into four layers. Which topics belong to the 'Building Apps that leverage LLMs' layer?
options: Transformers and training procedures
Fine tuning, prompt engineering, and evals
RAG and agents
Requirements engineering and code generation
questions.explanation: The four layers are: LLM technologies (transformers, training), Using LLMs (fine tuning, prompt engineering, evals), Building Apps that leverage LLMs (RAG, agents), and Using LLMs for Software Engineering (requirements, code generation).

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of a foundational model as described in the lecture?
options: Broad training on massive, diverse datasets such as books, websites, and code
Large scale, with billions of parameters and enormous training compute
Adaptability to many tasks using just instructions or a few examples
Purpose-built optimization for one narrow task, requiring retraining for each new use
questions.explanation: Foundational models are defined by broad training, scale, and adaptability; they are explicitly NOT purpose-built for one narrow task — that is what makes them a 'foundation' that can be adapted to many applications.

questions.stem: According to the lecture, what was the key contribution of the pioneering language-model work by Bengio, Hinton, and colleagues?
options: Inventing the transformer architecture with multi-head attention
Learning contextualized embeddings where the probability of the next word is a function of the embeddings of surrounding words
Building hand-written grammar rules that generate syntactically valid sentences
Introducing reinforcement learning from human feedback for alignment
questions.explanation: Bengio's neural probabilistic language model showed how to learn contextualized embeddings, making next-word probability a function of surrounding words' embeddings. Transformers and self-attention came later, and RLHF is a much more recent alignment technique.

questions.stem: What does self-attention — the core innovation of transformers — allow an LLM to do?
options: Dynamically weigh the importance of different words while processing the entire sequence simultaneously, capturing long-range dependencies
Compress the input into one fixed summary vector that is then read back word by word, as recurrent networks do
Retrieve relevant passages from an external knowledge base at generation time to ground its answers
Guarantee deterministic outputs by eliminating all sampling randomness from the decoding step
questions.explanation: Self-attention weighs the importance of different words in a sequence dynamically and processes the whole sequence simultaneously, which is what lets transformers capture long-range dependencies. Retrieval is a separate augmentation, and determinism is a sampling issue.

questions.stem: In the lecture's four 'dimensions' of an LLM, questions like 'Is the model open-source or closed-source?' and 'Is it customizable to your problem?' belong to which dimension?
options: Technical
Data
Governance
Post-training
questions.explanation: The governance dimension covers open-source vs. closed-source status and customizability. Technical covers architecture and size, data covers the training corpus, and post-training covers specializations like instruction following or alignment.

questions.stem: Which model-architecture family, as classified in the lecture, generates text by predicting the next token?
options: Autoencoding
Diffusion
Seq2Seq encoders
Autoregressive
questions.explanation: The lecture lists Autoregressive (predicting the next token) vs. Autoencoding vs. Seq2Seq architectures; autoregressive models are the ones that generate by predicting each next token in turn.

questions.stem: A model card states that a model has 96 layers and 64 attention heads. In this specification, 'attention heads' refers to…
options: the number of parallel attention mechanisms that exist in each layer
the total number of transformer blocks stacked in the model
the size of the vector representations used inside the model
the total count of learned weights, quoted in billions
questions.explanation: Attention heads are the parallel attention mechanisms within each layer. Stacked transformer blocks are the layer count, vector size is the embedding dimension, and total weights are the parameter count.

questions.stem: A team has budget to grow model size (N), training data (D), or training compute (C). According to the scaling study cited in the lecture, performance scales BEST when…
options: everything is invested in N, since parameter count dominates the other factors
everything is invested in D, since data is always the binding bottleneck
all three factors N, D, and C are scaled up in parallel
N and C are scaled while D is deliberately held constant
questions.explanation: The study found 'bigger is better' for models, data, and compute — and that performance scales best when all three are scaled together in parallel, not when one factor is grown in isolation.

questions.stem: Which statement best captures the 'smooth power laws' finding about LLM scaling described in the lecture?
options: Test loss has a power-law relationship with each of N, D, and C when not bottlenecked by the other two, over six-plus orders of magnitude
Test loss improves linearly with parameter count until the training data is exhausted, then stops improving
Test loss drops exponentially with compute and then abruptly plateaus at a fixed loss floor observed in the study
The power-law trend was observed to break down clearly at the upper end of the studied scales
questions.explanation: Performance follows a power law with each scale factor when the other two are not bottlenecks, with trends spanning more than six orders of magnitude and no observed deviation at the upper end (though loss must eventually flatten before zero).

questions.stem: A benchmark result shows a much smaller model beating a leading large model on a specific task. Based on the lecture, the most likely explanation is that…
options: small models generally outperform large models on most benchmarks
scaling laws stop applying once models pass a certain parameter count
the large model's bigger context window slowed it down and reduced accuracy
smart prompt engineering and related techniques can sometimes defeat the bigger models
questions.explanation: The lecture notes that while 'the big guys usually win', smart prompt engineering and other techniques can sometimes let smaller models defeat larger ones. This is an exception to, not a refutation of, the general scaling trend.

questions.stem: An LLM assistant answers well about older frameworks but knows nothing about a library released last year. Which property of the model's training data most directly explains this?
options: Its modality — the model was trained on text only, not vision or audio
Its cutoff date — the training corpus contains no data past a certain point in time
Its deduplication — repeated documents were removed during data cleansing
Its volume — the corpus was too small to include software topics at all
questions.explanation: The data dimension includes a cutoff date: the model cannot know about anything published after its training data was collected. Modality, deduplication, and raw volume would not selectively erase only recent knowledge.

questions.stem: In the lecture's list of post-training purposes, 'reasoning' post-training means…
options: re-running pre-training on a larger and more diverse corpus
preventing the model from producing harmful or unethical content
teaching the model to show its reasoning process step-by-step before providing final answers
connecting the model to external knowledge bases at query time
questions.explanation: Reasoning post-training teaches models to show step-by-step reasoning before the final answer. Preventing harmful content is value alignment, and using external knowledge bases is retrieval augmentation.

questions.stem: A biomedical company wants an LLM that gives specialized answers about drug interactions. Which post-training approach from the lecture most directly fits, with BioGPT as a cited example?
options: Task-specific post-training that augments the model for a particular domain
Intention and value alignment to prevent dangerous content
Instruction-following adaptation so the model obeys user requests
Retrieval augmentation from external document stores
questions.explanation: Task-specific post-training adapts models to domains like biomedicine (BioGPT), law (CaseLaw-GPT), or code (Codex, CodeLlama). Alignment, instruction following, and retrieval augmentation serve different purposes.

questions.stem: According to the lecture's footnote, how were the DeepSeek Coder models trained?
options: Fine-tuned from Llama weights on an English-only code corpus
Trained exclusively on source code, with natural language removed
Trained mostly on natural language, with a small fraction of code added
Trained from scratch on roughly 87% code and 13% natural language in English and Chinese
questions.explanation: DeepSeek Coder models were trained from scratch on 87% code and 13% natural language (English and Chinese), pre-trained on 12T tokens — not fine-tuned from another model and not code-only.

questions.stem: Your company uses a proprietary LLM through a paid API and asks the vendor whether your team can retrain the model's weights in-house. Per the lecture, closed-source LLMs…
options: can be fine-tuned by any paying customer who downloads the weights
can only be refined by the authoring organization, not by downstream customers
expose their weights for inspection but charge extra for modification rights
must be released as open source once they are commercially deployed
questions.explanation: Closed-source LLMs are proprietary, typically accessed via API for a fee, and can only be refined by the authoring organization. Downstream customers do not get the weights, unlike with open-source models.

questions.stem: A hospital must guarantee that prompts containing patient data are never seen by an external LLM provider. Which advantage of open-source LLMs from the lecture addresses this most directly?
options: Community contributions that improve the model over time
Fast out-of-the-box usability without hiring ML experts
On-premise deployment, so prompt and response data are seen only by the organization
Better benchmark performance backed by big-tech compute resources
questions.explanation: Open-source LLMs enable on-premise deployment, so data in prompts and responses stays inside the organization and is never sent to an LLM provider. Out-of-the-box usability and top benchmark performance are advantages of closed-source models.

questions.stem: A small startup with no machine-learning experts must ship an LLM-powered feature for a standard use-case within weeks. Based on the lecture's comparison, its best fit is…
options: a closed-source LLM via API, used out-of-the-box for fast time-to-value
an open-source model fine-tuned in-house on the startup's own data
training a small foundational model from scratch on domain data
an open-source model self-hosted on the startup's own GPU cluster
questions.explanation: Closed-source LLMs offer usability and time-to-value: they work out of the box for standard use-cases without hiring ML experts or managing powerful machines. Open-source routes require in-house expertise and compute the startup lacks.

questions.stem: According to the Goldman Sachs research cited in the lecture, which occupation is among those at HIGHEST risk of displacement by AI in the coming years?
options: Air traffic controllers
Computer programmers
Radiologists
Photographers
questions.explanation: Goldman Sachs lists computer programmers, accountants, legal assistants, and similar roles at highest risk; air traffic controllers, radiologists, pharmacists, and photographers are among those at least risk.

questions.stem: The CACM article quoted in the lecture argues that the 'new atomic unit of computation' is becoming…
options: a von Neumann machine of processor, memory, and I/O system
a formally verified instruction set with a static type system
a massive, pre-trained, highly adaptive AI model
a distributed cluster of general-purpose GPU accelerators
questions.explanation: The article argues the atomic unit of computation is no longer a von Neumann machine but a massive, pre-trained, highly adaptive AI model — a shift away from computation as a predictable process amenable to static analysis and formal proof.

questions.stem: Which statement best matches the outlook on the future of software engineering presented in the lecture?
options: Engineers will still be needed for the foreseeable future, but SE will change dramatically and demands new skills
Programming will certainly disappear within a few years, so studying SE fundamentals is no longer worthwhile
Generative AI will remain a minor productivity aid with little real effect on how engineers do their work
The changes will affect only hardware engineering, leaving software development practices unchanged
questions.explanation: The lecture concludes that no one can tell the future, but its working view is that software engineers will still be needed while the profession changes dramatically — the course exists to prepare students for that change and the new skills it requires.

questions.stem: At its core, what is a language model, according to the recitation?
options: A database of memorized sentences retrieved by keyword matching
A next-token predictor that assigns probabilities to possible continuations
A rule engine encoding grammar rules hand-written by linguists
A search engine that ranks existing web documents by relevance
questions.explanation: The recitation's core idea is 'Language Model = Next Token Predictor': given text like 'The cat sat on the ___', the model assigns probabilities to candidate next tokens rather than retrieving stored sentences or applying hand-written rules.

source.file: Recitation 1 - Intro to GenAI  LLM Basics.pdf
source.title: Recitation 1 — Intro to GenAI & LLM Basics

questions.stem: Which statement about tokens is accurate, per the recitation?
options: A token can be a whole word, part of a word, punctuation, or even a space
Tokens always correspond to exactly one complete word of text
Punctuation marks are stripped out before tokenization and never become tokens
Tokens are the embedding vectors the model uses internally to represent words
questions.explanation: A token is the basic unit of text a language model processes, and it can be a whole word, a part of a word, punctuation, or a space — 'Hello world!' becomes ['Hello', ' world', '!']. Tokens are units of text, not embedding vectors.

source.file: Recitation 1 - Intro to GenAI  LLM Basics.pdf
source.title: Recitation 1 — Intro to GenAI & LLM Basics

questions.stem: A tokenizer splits 'running' into 'run' + 'ning' and 'ChatGPT' into 'Chat' + 'GPT'. Why is this subword approach useful for a language model?
options: It guarantees each word maps to exactly one token, which simplifies decoding
It halves the billing cost, because providers charge less for subword tokens
It can represent rare or unseen words by combining smaller known units
It removes the need for any vocabulary, treating all text as raw characters
questions.explanation: Subword tokenization breaks words into smaller reusable units, so new or rare words can still be represented by combining known pieces while keeping the vocabulary manageable. Character-level tokenization, not subword, dispenses with word units entirely.

source.file: Recitation 1 - Intro to GenAI  LLM Basics.pdf
source.title: Recitation 1 — Intro to GenAI & LLM Basics

questions.stem: According to the recitation, in which situation is character-level tokenization (individual characters as tokens) most commonly used?
options: English legal contracts containing unusually long words
Source code with long camelCase identifiers
Emoji-heavy chat messages, where each emoji spans many bytes
Languages with very large character sets, such as Chinese
questions.explanation: The recitation notes that using individual characters as tokens is common in languages with large character sets, like Chinese, where each character already carries substantial meaning.

source.file: Recitation 1 - Intro to GenAI  LLM Basics.pdf
source.title: Recitation 1 — Intro to GenAI & LLM Basics

questions.stem: You paste an entire large design document into a single prompt and the model cannot handle the request at all. Which token-related concept explains this failure?
options: Cost — each additional token increases the per-request price
The context window — models can only process a limited number of tokens at once
Vocabulary gaps — long documents contain words the tokenizer has never seen
Sampling instability — probability distributions degrade on very long prompts
questions.explanation: Models can only process a limited number of tokens at once — the context window. If the input is too long, the model cannot handle it. Cost affects your bill, not whether the request is possible.

source.file: Recitation 1 - Intro to GenAI  LLM Basics.pdf
source.title: Recitation 1 — Intro to GenAI & LLM Basics

questions.stem: You rewrite a verbose prompt so it uses half as many tokens while conveying the same content. Based on the recitation, what should you expect?
options: Lower cost and faster responses, since both scale with the number of tokens processed
Lower cost but noticeably slower responses, since short prompts are harder to parse
Identical cost, because API billing is per request, but somewhat faster responses
Reduced output quality, because models are optimized for long, detailed prompts
questions.explanation: Tokens drive both cost (billing is based on tokens processed) and latency (more tokens mean more computation and slower responses), so halving the token count reduces both.

source.file: Recitation 1 - Intro to GenAI  LLM Basics.pdf
source.title: Recitation 1 — Intro to GenAI & LLM Basics

questions.stem: For 'The cat sat on the ___' the model assigns mat=0.45, chair=0.25, moon=0.01. If we ALWAYS pick the highest-probability token instead of sampling, what happens?
options: Responses become more diverse, because the top token changes on every run
The probabilities are redistributed so all remaining tokens become equally likely
Generation becomes deterministic — the same prompt always yields the same continuation
Generation speeds up, because the model skips computing the probability distribution
questions.explanation: Always taking the argmax removes randomness: the same prompt produces the same output every time, at the cost of variety. The model still computes the full probability distribution — greedy selection only changes how a token is chosen from it.

source.file: Recitation 1 - Intro to GenAI  LLM Basics.pdf
source.title: Recitation 1 — Intro to GenAI & LLM Basics

questions.stem: Per the recitation, LOWERING the sampling temperature makes the model…
options: more creative and more likely to pick surprising, low-probability tokens
respond faster, because fewer output tokens are generated per request
ignore the system message and rely only on the latest user message
more deterministic, focusing its choices on the most probable tokens
questions.explanation: Temperature controls how 'bold' sampling is: lowering it makes responses more deterministic and concentrated on the most probable tokens, while raising it produces more unexpected responses. It does not affect speed or behavior instructions.

source.file: Recitation 1 - Intro to GenAI  LLM Basics.pdf
source.title: Recitation 1 — Intro to GenAI & LLM Basics

questions.stem: You want the model to consistently behave like a strict professor across a conversation. What does the recitation recommend?
options: Use system instructions, prompt wording, context, and examples — temperature only controls randomness
Raise the temperature, since a bolder sampling policy produces a stricter tone
Lower the temperature to 0, which forces the model to adopt any requested persona
Behavior cannot be changed at inference time; only fine-tuning the weights works
questions.explanation: Temperature controls randomness, not behavior. Behavior is shaped by system instructions, prompt wording, context, and examples — e.g., a system message like 'You are a strict professor.'

source.file: Recitation 1 - Intro to GenAI  LLM Basics.pdf
source.title: Recitation 1 — Intro to GenAI & LLM Basics

questions.stem: In a chat-API conversation, where should the instruction 'You are a helpful code reviewer; always answer in JSON' go so that it sets the rules for the whole conversation?
options: In the assistant role, so the model restates the rule in each reply
In the system role, which sets behavior, context, and rules for the assistant
Appended to every user message, since the most recent tokens dominate
In a metadata field outside the conversation, next to the temperature setting
questions.explanation: Of the three role types, the system role sets behavior, context, and rules for the assistant; the user role carries the task or question, and the assistant role is the model's generated response.

source.file: Recitation 1 - Intro to GenAI  LLM Basics.pdf
source.title: Recitation 1 — Intro to GenAI & LLM Basics

questions.stem: The recitation maps the three chat roles onto conceptual layers. Which mapping is correct?
options: System = generation layer, user = configuration layer, assistant = request layer
System = request layer, user = configuration layer, assistant = generation layer
System = configuration layer, user = request layer, assistant = generation layer
System = configuration layer, user = generation layer, assistant = request layer
questions.explanation: System messages configure the assistant (configuration layer), user messages carry the request (request layer), and assistant messages are the generated output (generation layer).

source.file: Recitation 1 - Intro to GenAI  LLM Basics.pdf
source.title: Recitation 1 — Intro to GenAI & LLM Basics

questions.stem: A teammate sends a system/user/assistant-formatted conversation to a base (non-chat) model and gets confusing output. Per the recitation's note, the most likely reason is that…
options: base models require temperature 0 before they can parse role tags
base models accept only assistant-role messages as their input
role tags must be converted to uppercase before base models recognize them
role-based formatting is specific to chat-optimized models; base models expect a single plain prompt
questions.explanation: The recitation notes the system/user/assistant format is specific to chat-optimized models — base models or simple instruction models often operate with a single prompt instead of role-based messages.

source.file: Recitation 1 - Intro to GenAI  LLM Basics.pdf
source.title: Recitation 1 — Intro to GenAI & LLM Basics

questions.stem: According to the recitation, what actually happens when your application 'calls an LLM' like GPT-4?
options: The model is downloaded once and then runs inference locally in your process
The provider streams the model weights so your client performs the computation
Your prompt is queued for a human operator who curates the model's response
The client sends an HTTP request with JSON to a remote server that runs the model and returns a JSON response
questions.explanation: We do not run GPT-4 locally: the client sends an HTTP request (JSON) to a remote server, the server runs the model, and it sends back an HTTP response (JSON). The weights never leave the provider.

source.file: Recitation 1 - Intro to GenAI  LLM Basics.pdf
source.title: Recitation 1 — Intro to GenAI & LLM Basics

questions.stem: Per the recitation, what distinguishes OPEN models such as LLaMA, Mistral, and Gemma from closed models?
options: Their weights and architecture are publicly released, so they can be downloaded, inspected, and modified
They are always free to use for any commercial purpose without any restrictions
They can only be accessed through a paid web interface hosted by the vendor
They are always more powerful than closed models on standard benchmarks
questions.explanation: Open models are defined by publicly released weights and architecture — they can be run locally, inspected, and modified. Being open does not mean always free or more powerful; closed models are often the most capable.

source.file: Recitation 1 - Intro to GenAI  LLM Basics.pdf
source.title: Recitation 1 — Intro to GenAI & LLM Basics

questions.stem: As a rule of thumb given in class, one token corresponds to approximately…
options: one English character
four English characters
one full English sentence
ten English words
questions.explanation: The quiz teaches that a token corresponds to approximately 4 English characters. A token is a unit of text — it may include punctuation — and is not the same thing as an embedding vector or a whole sentence.

questions.stem: Model A advertises a context window twice as large as Model B's. Which conclusion is usually valid?
options: Model A will provide more precise answers than Model B on the same input
Model B can accept more input tokens per request than Model A
You can provide Model A with more input in a single request than Model B
Model A will always be cheaper to run per request than Model B
questions.explanation: A bigger context window means the model can accept more input tokens at once. It does not imply more precise answers — answer quality depends on the model itself, and a larger window generally costs more compute, not less.

questions.stem: At one decoding step, logit A is highest and logit B is second highest. You change the temperature from 1.0 to 1.5. What happens to the sampling probabilities?
options: They stay exactly the same, because temperature only affects generation speed
A becomes even more likely to be picked relative to B than before
B's probability becomes exactly 1.5 times larger than it was before
B now has more chance than before of being picked over A
questions.explanation: In softmax with temperature, a higher T shrinks the differences between logits, flattening the distribution toward uniform — so the runner-up B gains probability relative to A. The change is not a simple 1.5x multiplication.

questions.stem: You set an LLM's temperature to 0 and re-send the identical prompt several times, expecting byte-identical outputs. According to the class discussion, what is the accurate expectation?
options: The model becomes 'more' deterministic, but identical outputs are still not guaranteed
The model becomes fully deterministic — the same prompt always yields the same result
The model stops generating entirely, since sampling requires a positive temperature
The API rejects the request, because temperature 0 is mathematically undefined
questions.explanation: Temperature 0 makes generation much more deterministic by concentrating on the top token, but in practice the LLM can still be non-deterministic, so identical outputs across runs are not guaranteed.

questions.stem: A paper discussed in class ('Optimizing Large Language Model Hyperparameters for Code Generation') examined four hyperparameters. What did it conclude about generating correct code?
options: None of the four hyperparameters had a significant impact on code correctness
The temperature parameter had the most significant impact on code correctness
The Top-P parameter had the most significant impact on code correctness
Temperature and Top-P influenced code correctness equally strongly
questions.explanation: The paper concluded that temperature has the most significant impact on whether correct code is generated — more than Top-P or the other hyperparameters examined.
